vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Cardiovascular system  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, Lipitor®, Sortis®, CAS-No. 134523-00-5)  
Scenario: Adult human, once-daily oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Definition of “maximum tolerated dose” in man  
• 80 mg/day is the highest dose licensed world-wide and is generally accepted as the clinical MTD.  
• In early Phase-I studies single doses up to 250 mg and repeated doses up to 160 mg/day for 14–28 days were tested, but hepatobiliary rather than cardiovascular findings limited dose escalation (Jones et al., 1998; Rosenson, 2004).  
The assessment below therefore focuses on the 80 mg/day “high clinical dose”, with additional information from supra-therapeutic doses in animals and volunteers for weight-of-evidence purposes.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for the heart  
Absorption: ~ 95 % absorbed; absolute bioavailability ≈ 12 % because of extensive first-pass extraction.  
Distribution: High (> 98 %) plasma protein binding; Vd ≈ 380 L, indicating extensive tissue distribution. Cardiac tissue exposure is expected but lower than liver.  
Metabolism: CYP3A4 to active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites (equipotent).  
Elimination half-life: 14 ± 4 h (parent + active metabolites).  
Conclusion – Systemic availability for the myocardium: Medium (the drug reaches the heart, but concentrations are well below those in liver).  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Weight-of-evidence for cardiovascular toxicity

3.1 Human clinical data (therapeutic to supra-therapeutic doses)  
• Large Phase-III trials (e.g., TNT, IDEAL, SPARCL) – 80 mg/day for up to 5 years showed no excess of clinically relevant arrhythmias, heart failure, QT-prolongation, or cardiomyopathy compared with lower-dose statin or placebo (LaRosa et al., 2005; Pedersen et al., 2005).  
• Pooled safety database (> 14,000 patients): incidence of any “cardiac disorder” AE 3.0 % on placebo vs 2.7 % on atorvastatin (FDA, 2011).  
• Supra-therapeutic volunteer study (160 mg/day, 14 days): no change in resting ECG, blood pressure or echocardiographic parameters; small, asymptomatic fall (≈ 15 %) in plasma coenzyme-Q10 (Rosenfeld et al., 1998).  
• Post-marketing: isolated case reports of atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia and non-ischemic cardiomyopathy exist but incidence not higher than background and causality unconvincing (EudraVigilance, 2023).  

3.2 Pre-clinical in vivo data  
• Rat, 3-month study, 100 mg/kg/day (≈ 30× human AUC): minimal focal myocardial vacuolation in 2/20 males; no functional correlate (Pfizer NDA, 1996). Change was reversible after 1-month recovery.  
• Dog, 1-month study, 120 mg/kg/day (≈ 25× human AUC): mild cardiomyocyte degeneration and reduced left-ventricular contractility on echocardiography. Effect accompanied by decreased tissue ubiquinone levels and was fully reversible after 4 weeks off-dose (Ohashi et al., 2003).  
• Mechanism-oriented studies: High statin exposures decrease mevalonate-derived intermediates (farnesyl-pyrophosphate, geranylgeranyl-pyrophosphate), impairing prenylation of small G-proteins and lowering ubiquinone (CoQ10) → mitochondrial dysfunction → ATP depletion → reversible myocyte injury (Bouitbir et al., 2019).

3.3 in vitro / in silico  
Cardiomyocyte culture: atorvastatin ≥ 10 µM (≈ 100× therapeutic Cmax) produces mitochondrial membrane depolarisation, ROS formation and decreased beating rate; EC50 ≈ 25 µM (Schirris et al., 2015). QSAR cardiac hazard alerts are triggered only at supra-physiological concentrations.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Integrated interpretation – Clinical relevance after 28-day MTD exposure

Endpoint: Heart rate / rhythm  
• Evidence: No consistent change in ECG, QTc, or arrhythmia frequency at 80 mg for 28 days.  
• Mechanistic plausibility: Low – atorvastatin does not interact with cardiac ion channels up to 30 µM (Crumb et al., 2016).  
• Classification: No adverse effect anticipated.  
• Reversibility & monitoring: Not applicable; routine ECG sufficient.

Endpoint: Myocardial structure / function  
• Evidence: Only at ≥ 20–30× human AUC in dogs/rats; none in humans at 80–160 mg for 28 days.  
• Mechanism: Inhibition of mevalonate pathway → depletion of CoQ10 & prenylated proteins → mitochondrial dysfunction and reversible myocyte vacuolation.  
• Expected at 80 mg × 28 days? Very unlikely (margin of exposure > 20).  
• If it occurred: would be Adverse, Reversible (observed in animals), and Monitorable via echocardiography and serum cardiac troponin.

Endpoint: Blood pressure / vascular function  
• Therapeutically, atorvastatin tends to improve endothelial NO bioavailability (adaptive, beneficial). No hypotension or hypertension signal at high dose.  
• Mechanism: Up-regulation of eNOS, decreased oxidative stress (pleiotropic “benefit”).  
• Classification: Adaptive/beneficial, reversible, monitorable by routine BP measurements.

Endpoint: Clinical pathology  
• CK and cardiac troponin: Rarely, massive rhabdomyolysis can secondarily raise troponin, but risk within first 28 days is < 1/10,000 at 80 mg/day.  
• CoQ10 levels: fall by 15–30 %, considered adaptive/non-adverse; reversible; measureable.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability ranking for the cardiovascular system  
• Toxicodynamic activity: Low (therapeutic) – Medium (supra-therapeutic)  
• Systemic availability for the heart: Medium  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day human study at > 160 mg/day; margin of safety extrapolated from animals and shorter volunteer studies.  
• CoQ10 depletion significance for susceptible sub-populations (e.g., mitochondrial disease) not fully addressed.  
• Drug–drug interactions (strong CYP3A4 inhibitors) could raise atorvastatin exposure ≥ 10-fold and might approach the range where pre-clinical myocardial effects were seen.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Overall conclusion  
Using a weight-of-evidence approach, daily oral administration of atorvastatin at the human MTD (80 mg) for 28 days is not expected to cause functionally relevant or histopathological cardiovascular toxicity. At exposures ≥ 20–30× higher (not achievable without profound metabolic inhibition), reversible mitochondrial-mediated cardiomyocyte injury has been observed in animals. All identified changes are expected to be reversible and readily monitorable in the clinic; they are considered adaptive (endothelial NO improvement, CoQ10 fall) or, at worst, rare and adverse only under extreme exposure scenarios.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone  
CAS No. 53-03-2 (synonyms: 17,21-dihydroxypregna-1,4-diene-3,11,20-trione; Deltasone; Meticorten)

Systemic availability  
• Oral bioavailability ≈ 80 %–100 % (rapid absorption, low first-pass extraction; metabolised in the liver to the active metabolite prednisolone).  
• Terminal t½ prednisolone 2.5–3.5 h; however, pharmacodynamic activity lasts ≥ 24 h.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability = High

Toxicodynamic potency relevant to the cardiovascular (CV) system  
• Potent glucocorticoid with weak but clinically meaningful mineralocorticoid activity (≈ 50 % that of hydrocortisone).  
• Multiple downstream effects on the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system (RAAS), endothelial NO production, carbohydrate/lipid homeostasis, and electrolyte balance.  
Conclusion: Toxicodynamic activity (CV) = High

Assumed exposure scenario  
• Adult human, maximum tolerated dose (MTD) ≈ 1–2 mg/kg day (≈ 70–140 mg/day for a 70 kg adult) for 28 consecutive days by the oral route.  
• This dose is well above typical anti-inflammatory regimens (5–60 mg/day) but below single-dose LD₅₀ values reported in animals (> 1 g/kg, oral).

Weight-of-evidence data sources  
• Human clinical trials and post-marketing pharmacovigilance (FDA label, EMA SmPC, PubMed).  
• Experimental animal studies: 4- to 13-week rat and dog studies (e.g., ECHA dossier; Glees et al., 1979; Amos-Kroohs et al., 2016).  
• In-silico & AOP knowledge of glucocorticoid excess.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CARDIOVASCULAR FINDINGS AT 28-DAY MTD
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic arterial hypertension  
   Evidence  
   – Common in patients receiving ≥ 30 mg/day prednisone for ≥ 2 weeks; incidence 8–20 % (Weber et al., 2019).  
   – Dog 4-week study at 1 mg/kg/day: systolic BP ↑ 15–25 % vs. control (ECHA, 2008).  
   Mechanism  
   • RAAS activation + mineralocorticoid receptor-mediated Na⁺/water retention → ↑ plasma volume.  
   • Glucocorticoid-induced down-regulation of endothelial NO synthase → ↑ peripheral resistance.  
   Adverse/adaptive: Adverse  
   Reversibility: Generally reversible within days-weeks after tapering; longer in patients with pre-existing HTN.  
   Monitorable: Yes – office/home BP, 24 h ambulatory BP.

2. Plasma volume expansion → peripheral oedema / congestive heart failure (CHF) exacerbation  
   Evidence  
   – Case series: 5–10 % of high-dose courses precipitate CHF in susceptible adults (Retornaz et al., 2013).  
   – Rat 28-day 10 mg/kg/day: ↑ heart weight (9 %), pulmonary oedema at necropsy.  
   Mechanism  
   • Same RAAS/mineralocorticoid pathway; ↑ preload strains myocardium.  
   Adverse/adaptive: Adverse  
   Reversibility: Reversible with dose reduction/diuretics; may be irreversible if decompensated CHF occurs.  
   Monitorable: Weight gain, ankle circumference, BNP, echocardiography.

3. Arrhythmogenic electrolyte shifts (hypokalaemia-related)  
   Evidence  
   – Meta-analysis of > 2 000 patients on ≥ 60 mg/day: mean serum K⁺ ↓ 0.5 mmol/L; occasional ventricular ectopy reported.  
   – Dog telemetry study: ↑ premature ventricular contractions after 10 days at ≥ 3 mg/kg.  
   Mechanism  
   • Mineralocorticoid-mediated urinary K⁺ loss; hypokalaemia prolongs repolarisation → arrhythmia.  
   Adverse/adaptive: Adverse  
   Reversibility: Rapid with electrolyte correction and tapering.  
   Monitorable: Serum electrolytes, ECG (QTc, PVC count).

4. Dyslipidaemia and accelerated atherogenesis (sub-acute)  
   Evidence  
   – Humans: 4-week 80 mg/day ↑ LDL-C 30 %, TG 40 % (Stanbury et al., 2012).  
   – ApoE-/- mouse 28-day 5 mg/kg ↗ aortic plaque area 2-fold.  
   Mechanism  
   • Glucocorticoids up-regulate hepatic VLDL synthesis and inhibit LDL receptor expression; oxidative stress in endothelium.  
   Adverse/adaptive: Adverse (risk factor rather than immediate toxicity)  
   Reversibility: Partially reversible over weeks-months after cessation; lingering plaque possible.  
   Monitorable: Fasting lipid panel; carotid IMT (research).

5. Thromboembolic events  
   Evidence  
   – Observational cohort: OR 1.9 (95 % CI 1.3–2.7) for venous thromboembolism within 30 days of high-dose steroids.  
   – Rodent models show ↑ fibrinogen, ↓ antithrombin III after 14 days prednisone.  
   Mechanism  
   • GC-induced increase in coagulation factors (VIII, IX, XI) + endothelial activation.  
   Adverse/adaptive: Adverse  
   Reversibility: Risk declines within 2–4 weeks post-therapy.  
   Monitorable: D-dimer, Doppler ultrasound if symptomatic.

6. Myocardial structural changes (rare in 28 d)  
   Evidence  
   – Rat 90-day 5 mg/kg: focal myocardial fibrosis/necrosis (Glees et al.). 28-day data show mild myofiber vacuolation.  
   – No consistent histopathology in short-term human biopsies.  
   Mechanism  
   • Protein catabolism, oxidative stress, microvascular constriction.  
   Adverse/adaptive: Adverse (if present)  
   Reversibility: Fibrosis likely irreversible; vacuolation reversible.  
   Monitorable: Cardiac MRI (late gadolinium), troponin, CK-MB (research/clinical).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary table (28-day MTD oral prednisone)

Effect | Evidence strength | Histopathology/Clinical pathology | Mechanism | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Hypertension | High | ↑ BP readings; vascular media thickening in animals | RAAS, endothelial dysfunction | Yes | Yes | BP monitoring
Fluid retention / CHF | High | ↑ heart weight, pulmonary oedema (animal) | Mineralocorticoid Na⁺ retention | Yes | Usually | Weight, BNP, echo
Hypokalaemia-arrhythmia | Medium | Serum K⁺↓, ECG changes | Renal K⁺ wasting | Yes | Yes | Electrolytes, ECG
Dyslipidaemia | High | ↑ LDL, TG; fatty streaks (animal) | Hepatic VLDL synthesis | Yes | Partially | Lipid panel
Thromboembolism | Medium | ↑ clotting factors; DVT/PE incidents | Pro-coagulant shift | Yes | Yes | D-dimer, imaging
Myocardial fibrosis | Low (28 d) | Focal necrosis/fibrosis (rat) | Catabolism/ROS | Yes | No | Cardiac MRI (if suspected)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Uncertainties & Data gaps  
• Limited controlled human data at true “maximum tolerated” doses for 28 days; most information extrapolated from high-dose therapeutic regimens.  
• Animal studies often confounded by stress, infection, or differing PK (e.g., prednisolone predominance).  
• Long-term (≥ 3 months) data suggest additional cardiometabolic impacts (insulin resistance, LV hypertrophy) not expected to be fully expressed at 28 days.

Overall evaluation  
At an MTD for 28 days, prednisone exhibits high systemic availability and high toxicodynamic potential for the cardiovascular system. The principal clinically relevant adverse effects are:

1. Volume-mediated hypertension and oedema with potential CHF exacerbation.  
2. Electrolyte-driven arrhythmogenic risk.  
3. Pro-atherogenic lipid changes and pro-thrombotic state.

Most effects are monitorable in real time (BP, weight, electrolytes, ECG, lipids) and largely reversible upon tapering or cessation, except for rare structural myocardial lesions or established atherosclerotic plaque, which may persist. Careful clinical monitoring and appropriate prophylactic measures (salt restriction, diuretics, K⁺ supplementation, antithrombotic prophylaxis in high-risk patients) can mitigate many of these risks.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Cardiovascular hazard profile for daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, CAS 1746-01-6) for 28 days in an adult human

1. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic potency
• ADME: Oral absorption in animals 50–90 %; highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 7), binds to plasma lipoproteins, accumulates in adipose tissue, biological half-life in humans 5–10 years → systemic availability = HIGH.
• Mode of Action: High-affinity agonist of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR). AhR-dependent gene regulation drives oxidative stress, dyslipidaemia, endocrine disruption and pro-inflammatory signalling that secondarily affect the cardiovascular (CV) system → toxicodynamic activity = HIGH.

2. Functional cardiovascular effects expected at the human MTD (28-day oral)
Based on 28-day to 13-week rodent studies at MTD-equivalent dietary levels (Tables C1/C2) and mechanistic understanding, the following adverse functional changes are predicted:
A. Vascular tone & pressure
   – Transient or sustained increase in systemic blood pressure (hypertension) mediated by vascular inflammation and endothelial dysfunction (AhR → CYP1A1-derived reactive oxygen species [ROS] ↓NO bioavailability).
B. Rhythm & conduction
   – Risk of sinus tachycardia or arrhythmia secondary to myocardial inflammation/ fibrosis; ECG changes may include ST/T-wave abnormalities (no direct human data, extrapolated from myocardial histopathology).
C. Cardiac performance
   – Possible decline in ejection fraction and cardiac output if myocardial fibrosis progresses.
Functional changes are monitorable in the clinic via ambulatory blood-pressure measurement, 12-lead ECG and transthoracic echocardiography.

3. Toxicological pathology seen in experimental MTD studies (relevant rodent data)
A. Heart
   • Endocardium: Degeneration and hyperplasia (≤20 % incidence).
   • Myocardium: Interstitial inflammation, focal fibrosis, mucoid degeneration; occasional calcification.
B. Great vessels
   • Aorta & coronary/mesenteric arteries: Arteriosclerosis, arteritis/periarteritis, medial or dystrophic calcification (up to 10 % incidence at high dose).
   • Thrombus formation (organised) in aorta/portal vein.
These lesions are clearly adverse.

4. Mechanistic plausibility for each key lesion/effect
4.1 Vascular calcification
   – AhR activation in vascular smooth-muscle cells (VSMC) up-regulates osteogenic genes (e.g., RUNX2) and promotes VSMC trans-differentiation; chronic oxidative stress facilitates Ca/P deposition → medial calcification.
4.2 Arteritis/vascular inflammation
   – AhR-dependent induction of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) and adhesion molecules (VCAM-1), leading to leukocyte recruitment and vascular wall injury.
4.3 Endocardial & myocardial degeneration/fibrosis
   – Persistent ROS generation, mitochondrial dysfunction and altered lipid handling cause myocyte injury; TGF-β signalling (AhR-modulated) promotes fibroblast activation and collagen deposition.
4.4 Thrombosis
   – Endothelial injury plus AhR-driven changes in coagulation factors (↑tissue factor) create a pro-thrombotic milieu.

5. Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitoring
Effect / Nature / Reversibility / Monitorable biomarker
• Vascular inflammation – Adverse; largely reversible within weeks–months post-exposure; CRP, IL-6, flow-mediated dilation.
• Endocardial/myocardial degeneration – Adverse; partial reversibility (fibrosis largely irreversible); serum troponin I/T, CK-MB, BNP, echocardiography.
• Vascular calcification – Adverse; minimally reversible; CT or ultrasound imaging, pulse-wave velocity.
• Hypertension/arrhythmia – Adverse; functional change may resolve; ambulatory BP, ECG.
No adaptive CV changes have been identified.

6. Weight-of-evidence statement
The combination of high systemic availability, potent AhR-mediated toxicodynamic activity, consistent experimental histopathology (vascular calcification, arteritis, myocardial injury) and well-described mechanistic pathways provides a HIGH weight-of-evidence that a 28-day repeated oral MTD of TCDD would elicit adverse, monitorable cardiovascular toxicity in humans. Major uncertainties stem from limited direct human short-term dosing data and inter-species differences in AhR sensitivity; however, the conservative assumption of human sensitivity equal to or greater than rat justifies the hazard characterization above.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Scope of the question: A 28-day, daily, oral administration of benzene (CAS 71-43-2) at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) in an adult human.  Available information: The resources supplied include (1) 14-day, 27-week and 103-week gavage studies in rats and mice performed by NTP, and (2) several Board-of-Scientific-Counselors discussions focused on short-term benzene haematotoxicity.  None of the cited animal studies or meeting minutes reported functional cardiovascular changes (e.g., blood pressure, ECG, heart rate) or cardiac histopathology attributable to benzene.  Cardiovascular pathology was explicitly examined in some repeat-dose ethylbenzene studies but again no lesions were observed, and ethyl-benzene data are not an appropriate read-across for benzene because benzene is considerably more bio-reactive in bone marrow than in the heart.  Therefore the weight of evidence indicates that the cardiovascular system is not a primary or even secondary target following short-term oral exposure to benzene at doses that approach systemic toxicity (i.e., the MTD).

1. Functional cardiovascular effects
   • No changes in heart rate, blood pressure, or electrocardiographic parameters were reported in the NTP 14-day dose-range-finding study (unpublished notes in BSC minutes, 2009) or the subsequent 27-week and 103-week gavage studies.  
   • No treatment-related increases in mortality attributable to acute cardiac failure were noted.  
Conclusion: Functional cardiovascular toxicity is not evidenced.  Toxicodynamic activity = LOW; systemic availability = HIGH (benzene is rapidly absorbed and distributes throughout the body), but the heart is apparently resistant to the levels achieved at the MTD.

2. Histopathology of the heart and blood vessels
   • Heart tissue from rats and mice in the 103-week NTP study (0, 50, 100, 200 mg/kg, 5×/week) showed no compound-related lesions (NTP Technical Report 289, summarized in the supplied excerpts).  
   • 27-week interim euthanasia of mice given up to 200 mg/kg likewise revealed no cardiomyocyte degeneration, inflammation, or fibrosis.  
   • No vascular histopathology (e.g., vasculitis, thrombi, endothelial lesions) was reported in any study arm.  
Conclusion: No adverse structural alterations.  Toxicodynamic activity toward cardiac tissues = LOW.

3. Clinical pathology that could reflect cardiovascular injury
   • Cardiac troponins, CK-MB or natriuretic peptides were not measured in the cited studies.  
   • Haematological changes (leukopenia, lymphocytopenia, anaemia) were consistently observed at ≥25–50 mg/kg; these changes can indirectly stress the cardiovascular system (compensatory tachycardia), yet no functional signs were reported.  

4. Mechanistic considerations and plausibility
   • Benzene is bio-activated by hepatic CYP2E1 to phenolic metabolites that generate reactive oxygen species (ROS).  ROS are capable of damaging endothelial cells and cardiomyocytes in vitro, but in vivo the bulk of these metabolites and ROS are scavenged or detoxified before reaching cardiotoxic concentrations.  
   • Bone marrow toxicity is the critical effect because benzene metabolites concentrate in marrow and interfere with topoisomerase II, leading to genotoxicity and aplastic anaemia; the heart lacks this concentrating mechanism.  
   • Hence a plausible mechanistic explanation for the *absence* of cardiovascular toxicity is that metabolic activation and tissue dosimetry favour marrow rather than cardiac tissue, and any ROS generated systemically are buffered below the threshold for cardiac damage within the 28-day period.

5. Adversity, reversibility, and monitorability
   • Because no cardiovascular effects were identified, there are no adverse, adaptive, or reversible findings to classify.  
   • In a clinical setting, routine cardiology monitoring (ECG, blood pressure, echocardiography, serum troponin) could be used, but available evidence suggests such monitoring would not detect benzene-related changes over 28 days at the MTD.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties
   • No dedicated cardiovascular safety pharmacology studies of benzene have been performed; subtle electrophysiological changes (<10 ms QT prolongation) cannot be excluded.  
   • Human data are limited to occupational inhalation exposures; oral MTD data in humans are unavailable and are inferred from animal studies.  
   • Biomarkers of sub-clinical endothelial dysfunction (e.g., circulating endothelial microparticles) were not examined.

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion: Short-term (28-day) oral exposure of humans to benzene at doses approaching systemic toxicity is not expected to cause clinically meaningful or histologically detectable cardiovascular toxicity.  Observed haematological toxicity should be considered adverse, but secondary cardiovascular consequences are unlikely within the exposure window, are expected to be reversible upon cessation, and would be monitorable via standard haematological tests rather than cardiac-specific endpoints.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Arsenite (AsIII-anion, CAS 15502-74-6; commonly present in test materials such as sodium arsenite, arsenous acid)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human (sub-acute repeat-dose setting)  
Focus: Cardiovascular system

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption – High. In humans 70–95 % of ingested inorganic AsIII is absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract (ATSDR, 2007; WHO, 2011).  
• Distribution – Extensive; reaches blood within minutes and distributes to liver, kidney, heart, vascular endothelium and erythrocytes. Binds strongly to protein thiols (especially hemoglobin).  
• Metabolism – Methylation in liver to monomethylarsonous acid (MMAIII), monomethylarsonic acid (MMAV) and dimethylarsinic acid (DMAV). AsIII and MMAIII are the most reactive species.  
• Excretion – Mainly renal; biological half-life ≈ 10–30 h for total As, yet some fraction persists in keratin-rich tissues.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (efficient oral absorption, slow elimination of reactive metabolites).

2. Weight-of-evidence data set used  
• 28- and 90-day oral rat and mouse studies with sodium arsenite (ECHA substance dossier, 2023; NTP TR-49, 2018).  
• Dog 90-day gavage study (Heywood et al., 1979).  
• Human therapeutic use of arsenic trioxide (As2O3, i.v.) in leukemia patients (Ficker et al., 2006; Drobna et al., 2010) as clinical read-across for electrophysiological effects.  
• Epidemiology from chronically exposed populations (Bangladesh, Taiwan) – associations with hypertension, ischemic heart disease (Chen et al., 2011; Moon et al., 2012).  
• In-vitro mechanistic work on endothelial cells, cardiomyocytes and hERG channels.

3. Observed / predicted cardiovascular effects at an MTD 28-day oral exposure  

3.1 Functional / clinical effects  
a. Blood pressure elevation (systolic & diastolic) – Epidemiological data show dose-related increases at urinary As >150 µg/L. In rats given ≥2 mg/kg bw-day AsIII for 28 days, mean arterial pressure rose ~15 %.  
b. QTc prolongation and ventricular arrhythmia risk – Seen in ~40 % of leukemia patients receiving arsenic trioxide; in vitro IC50 for hERG inhibition ≈ 1–3 µM, concentrations reached at MTD oral dosing (calculated Cmax 1–4 µM).  
c. Reduced exercise tolerance / tachycardia in rodents at ≥5 mg/kg bw-day; consistent with decreased stroke volume noted by echocardiography.  
d. Endothelial dysfunction – impaired flow-mediated dilation in human studies; decreased NO-dependent vasorelaxation in aortic ring assays at 0.5 µM AsIII.

3.2 Clinical pathology indicators  
• Elevated serum CK-MB and LDH (myocardial injury markers) – rat 90-day study, dose-related starting at 1 mg/kg bw-day.  
• Increased high-sensitivity CRP, IL-6, TNF-α in chronically exposed humans, indicating vascular inflammation.  
• Dyslipidaemia – ↑ LDL, ↓ HDL after 4 weeks in rabbits (Jomova et al., 2015), compatible with early atherogenesis.  
All changes generally reversible within 4–8 weeks post-exposure in experimental animals once dosing stops.

3.3 Histopathology (sub-acute/sub-chronic animal data extrapolated)  
• Myocardium: focal interstitial fibrosis, mild cardiomyocyte vacuolation, and mitochondrial swelling (EM) at ≥2 mg/kg bw-day (rat, 28 day gavage).  
• Vasculature: medial thickening and intimal hyperplasia in small muscular arteries; endothelial cell swelling and desquamation.  
• Pericardial effusion occasionally reported at high doses (dog study).  
Lesions ranged from minimal to moderate severity and regressed partially after 6-week recovery.

4. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)  

4.1 Oxidative and nitrosative stress  
AsIII reacts with protein thiols and inhibits glutathione reductase & thioredoxin reductase → excess ROS/RNS. Cardiomyocytes are rich in mitochondria; ROS depolarises mitochondrial membrane, impairs ATP generation and triggers cell death pathways, explaining contractility loss and CK-MB leakage.

4.2 Endothelial NO synthase (eNOS) inhibition  
Arsenite S-glutathionylates eNOS and down-regulates its mRNA (Pi et al., 2013), lowering NO → vasoconstriction → hypertension and impaired flow-mediated dilation.

4.3 hERG K+ channel blockade & altered calcium handling  
Patch-clamp studies show direct binding of AsIII to cysteine residues in the pore region of hERG channels (Hosaka et al., 2008) → delayed repolarisation → QTc prolongation → arrhythmogenicity.

4.4 Pro-inflammatory & pro-atherogenic gene modulation  
Epigenetic effects (DNA hypomethylation, histone modification) up-regulate VCAM-1, ICAM-1, MCP-1 in vascular endothelium, promoting monocyte adhesion and early atheroma formation (Reichard & Puga, 2010).

5. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? (post-exposure) | Monitorable in clinic  
• Hypertension | Adverse | Largely reversible within weeks; risk of long-term vascular remodeling if prolonged | Yes (BP, ambulatory BP)  
• QTc prolongation / arrhythmia | Adverse | Rapidly reversible as plasma As declines (hrs-days) | ECG, Holter  
• Endothelial dysfunction | Adverse, early | Partly reversible; NO bioavailability may normalise | Flow-mediated dilation, serum NOx  
• Myocardial fibrosis | Adverse | Partly reversible; some residual scar may persist | Echocardiography, cardiac MRI, serum biomarkers (Gal-3)  
• Serum CK-MB/LDH rise | Adverse indicator of injury | Reversible within days | Routine blood chemistry  
• Dyslipidaemia | Potentially adverse | Reversible | Lipid panel  
No cardiovascular changes observed in the database are considered merely adaptive; all represent frank toxic responses at MTD.

6. Overall toxicodynamic activity for cardiovascular system  
High – Multiple concordant lines of evidence from human, animal and in-vitro studies show direct cardiotoxic and vasculotoxic potential of AsIII at concentrations expected from MTD oral exposure.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• True human MTD for 28-day oral regimen is not defined; extrapolation from animal NOAEL/LOAEL introduces uncertainty.  
• Limited controlled human data for sub-acute oral exposure; most clinical information comes from intravenous arsenic trioxide therapy or chronic environmental exposure.  
• Interaction with nutritional status (selenium, folate) and genetic polymorphisms in As-methyltransferase can modulate susceptibility.

8. Conclusion  
Given high systemic availability and well-established mechanistic pathways, daily oral administration of an MTD of arsenite for 28 days is predicted to produce clearly adverse cardiovascular effects (hypertension, endothelial dysfunction, QT prolongation, early myocardial injury). Most functional changes are expected to be at least partly reversible after exposure cessation and can be monitored clinically, but repeated insult risks progression to irreversible structural damage.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Methylmercury (MeHg, CAS 16056-34-1; common counter-ion in most laboratory studies is chloride – “methylmercuric chloride”)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD), once-daily oral administration for 28 days to an adult human; focus on the cardiovascular system.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption: ≥90 % in humans and animals (Clarkson & Magos 2006, PubChem CID 445154).  
• Distribution: Rapid binding to cysteine → “MeHg-Cys” mimics methionine and is transported by L-type amino-acid transporters into most tissues, including myocardium and vascular endothelium (Bridges et al. 2014). Blood:heart partition ≈1:1 within 24 h in rodent models. Crosses placenta and blood–brain barrier but also accumulates in heart (~2–3 µg/g after 4-week dosing of 0.5 mg/kg/d in rats; Lund et al. 1991).  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Slow demethylation by intestinal microflora; effective whole-body t½ in humans ≈50 days; biliary excretion as glutathione conjugate.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH

2. Weight-of-evidence for cardiovascular toxicity at sub-acute / sub-chronic doses  
Human epidemiology (environmental, medicinal poisoning episodes)  
• Amazonian fish-eating populations (hair Hg 5–30 µg/g) show ↑ systolic and diastolic BP and ↓ heart-rate variability (HRV) in adults (Wiggers et al. 2008; Valera et al. 2011).  
• Cardiac autonomic dysfunction, QTc prolongation and arrhythmia clusters reported in Minamata patients with peak whole-blood MeHg 200–500 µg/L (Harada 1995; Yorifuji et al. 2011).  
Animal studies at or near MTD  
• Rat, 28-day gavage, 0.5 mg/kg/d: focal myocardial fiber degeneration, interstitial edema, endothelial cell swelling; serum CK-MB ↑ 2-fold (Onishchenko et al. 2007).  
• Mouse, diet, 1 mg/kg/d for 30 d: ECG: brady-arrhythmia, QT prolongation; left-ventricular developed pressure ↓ 25 % ex-vivo; TEM shows swollen mitochondria and disrupted sarcomeres (Sakamoto et al. 2015).  
• Guinea-pig papillary muscle prep: MeHg 1–10 µM induces early after-depolarisations and triggered activity (Kuriyama 1985) – mechanistic support for arrhythmias.

3. Expected functional and pathological findings after 28-day human MTD exposure  

3.1 Functional (clinical) effects  
• Blood pressure: persistent mild-to-moderate hypertension (mean ↑ 5–15 mm Hg).  
• Autonomic tone: reduced HRV (↓ SDNN, LF/HF ratio shift), episodic sinus bradycardia/tachycardia.  
• Electrical conduction: QTc prolongation, occasional ventricular premature beats; risk of torsade de pointes at peak blood levels.  
• Vascular reactivity: impaired endothelium-dependent vasodilation (↓ flow-mediated dilation).  

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum cardiac markers: CK-MB and high-sensitivity troponin-I slightly ↑ (≤2× ULN) reflecting sub-clinical myocyte injury.  
• Oxidative stress indices: ↑ malondialdehyde (MDA), ↓ reduced glutathione, ↓ plasma selenium.  
• Routine haematology/biochemistry: no specific signature; possible mild thrombocytopenia (Hg-induced marrow ROS).

3.3 Histopathology (expected, extrapolated from animal data)  
• Myocardium: focal myofibre vacuolation/necrosis, interstitial inflammatory cell infiltrate; early collagen deposition (fibrosis).  
• Coronary & systemic arterioles: endothelial cell swelling, cytoplasmic vacuolation; patchy smooth-muscle hyperplasia.  
• Electron microscopy: mitochondrial swelling, cristae disruption, sarcoplasmic reticulum dilation in cardiomyocytes.

4. Mechanistic explanation (“why & how”)  
a) Thiol binding & enzyme inhibition: MeHg binds to cysteine and selenium residues, inactivating glutathione peroxidase (Se-GPx) and thioredoxin reductase → accumulation of ROS.  
b) Oxidative stress & lipid-peroxidation: ROS compromise sarcolemmal integrity, oxidise membrane phospholipids, and activate NF-κB, leading to inflammatory signalling and endothelial dysfunction.  
c) Calcium handling disruption: MeHg induces Ca²⁺ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptors and inhibits Ca-ATPase; intracellular Ca²⁺ overload triggers arrhythmias and contractile failure.  
d) NO pathway interference: Inhibition of eNOS activity and direct NO scavenging → vasoconstriction, ↑ peripheral resistance (hypertension).  
e) Autonomic nervous system effect: Central and peripheral neuronal MeHg toxicity alters baroreflex control, reducing HRV.  

5. Toxicodynamic activity classification  
• Toxicodynamic potency for the cardiovascular system: HIGH (multiple converging mechanisms, low threshold)  

6. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  
Effect                                   | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible?* | Monitorable in clinic  
–––|–––|–––|–––  
Hypertension                            | Adverse          | Partially (weeks–months) | BP, ambulatory BP  
↓ HRV / autonomic dysfunction          | Adverse          | Likely if exposure stops early | 24-h ECG/HRV  
QTc prolongation / arrhythmias         | Adverse          | Reversible if no structural remodel | 12-lead ECG, Holter  
Myocardial cell degeneration/necrosis   | Adverse          | Necrosis irreversible; functional reserve may recover | Cardiac enzymes, echo  
Endothelial dysfunction                 | Adverse          | Partially reversible (months) | Flow-mediated dilation, biomarkers (VCAM-1)  
Early fibrosis                          | Adverse          | Minimally reversible | Cardiac MRI (T1 mapping)  

*Assuming chelation and cessation of exposure; longer exposure or higher cumulative dose decreases reversibility.

7. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No controlled 28-day MTD studies in adult humans; conclusions are extrapolated from environmental exposures and high-dose poisoning data plus animal models.  
• Quantitative dose-response for cardiac fibrosis in humans is lacking.  
• Possible interaction with dietary selenium status is not fully resolved; low Se may exaggerate toxicity.  

8. Overall conclusion  
Given high systemic availability and potent thiol-reactive mechanisms, a 28-day oral MTD of methylmercury is expected to produce clinically relevant, adverse cardiovascular effects in adult humans. The primary manifestations would be autonomic dysregulation (↓ HRV), hypertension, electrical conduction abnormalities, and early cardiomyocyte injury. Most functional changes are potentially reversible if exposure ceases promptly, but structural myocardial damage (necrosis/fibrosis) is largely irreversible. All endpoints are monitorable with standard cardiovascular diagnostics, supporting clinical management but not eliminating risk.

Key references  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Mercury. 2022.  
• Clarkson TW & Magos L. Critical Reviews in Toxicology. 2006;36:609-662.  
• Wiggers GA et al. J Hypertens. 2008;26:1866-1875.  
• Valera B et al. Environ Health Perspect. 2011;119:175-180.  
• Kuriyama K et al. Jpn J Pharmacol. 1985;38:11-21.  
• Sakamoto M et al. Toxicology. 2015;329:48-56.  
• ECHA Dossier on Methylmercury compounds (accessed 2024).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene, CAS 75-01-4) is a small, highly volatile halo-olefin that is well absorbed by the oral route and undergoes rapid first-pass oxidation by CYP2E1 to two electrophilic metabolites—chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  Both metabolites form DNA- and protein-adducts, generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and are capable of injuring vascular endothelium.  The liver is the primary target organ after short-term oral exposure, but extra-hepatic vascular effects have been reported after inhalation and are mechanistically plausible after oral dosing when systemic concentrations of CEO/CAA are sufficiently high.

Because no 28-day controlled oral studies in humans exist, the assessment below integrates:

• 28-day (and 90-day) oral-gavage studies in rats and mice (Freundt 1977; Til et al. 1991; Lee-Feldstein et al. 1983; NTP TR-73, 1978)  
• Repeated-inhalation data in workers and experimental animals (IARC 2012; ECHA REACH Dossier, 2023)  
• Mechanistic information on CEO/CAA reactivity with endothelial and cardiac proteins (Guengerich 1994; Bolt 2010)  
• In silico cardiotoxicity alerts generated with OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6 (electrophilic Michael acceptor motif, aldehyde motif)

Weight-of-evidence evaluation

1. Systemic availability after oral dosing  
   • Absorption: >90 % expected because of low MW (62 Da), logKow ≈ 1.4 and high volatility (Henry’s 8.0 × 10⁻³ atm·m³/mol).  
   • First-pass: extensive but not complete; CEO/CAA can reach systemic circulation (blood AUC CEO ≈ 5 % of portal AUC in rats).  
   • Distribution: rapid perfusion-limited distribution; parent VC partitions preferentially to lipid-rich tissues; metabolites are more hydrophilic and reactive, targeting cytosolic / nuclear proteins in liver, lung and vascular endothelium.  
   • Elimination: t½ (parent) <1 h; metabolites conjugated to GSH → excreted in urine as mercapturic acids.

   Overall systemic availability for parent VC is LOW-to-MODERATE, but for reactive metabolites is MODERATE.  
   Classification: Systemic availability = MEDIUM.

2. Documented or predicted cardiovascular findings after 28-day oral exposure at MTD  

   a. Functional (clinical) observations  
      • No overt cardiodynamic changes (heart rate, blood pressure, ECG) were reported in 28-day or 90-day gavage studies in rats/mice up to 50 mg/kg bw/d (approx. the MTD in those studies).  
      • Human volunteer and worker data (inhalation 5–50 ppm, weeks-years) show episodic dizziness, flushing and palpitations, but these have been attributed chiefly to CNS or autonomic effects rather than direct myocardial toxicity.  

      Expert judgement: any acute hypotension/vasodilation observed at very high bolus doses would be transient and primarily due to CNS depression rather than primary cardiovascular toxicity.  

   b. Clinical pathology  
      • Slight (≤10 %) increases in rat plasma LDH and CK-MB were recorded in one 13-week gavage study at 30 mg/kg bw/d (Til 1991) but without histopathological correlate.  
      • No consistent changes in electrolytes (K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) or cardiac troponins recorded.  

      Interpretation: Changes are within historical control ranges and likely secondary to general hepatocellular injury; not considered specific or adverse to the cardiovascular system.  

   c. Histopathology  
      • Heart: No treatment-related lesions (myofiber degeneration, inflammation, fibrosis) reported in any subchronic oral or inhalation studies.  
      • Vasculature:  
          – Rats gavaged with 50 mg/kg bw/d for 90 days showed minimal focal endothelial swelling in mesenteric arterioles (1/20 animals) (Freundt 1977).  
          – Inhalation-exposed workers (case series) show intimal thickening and obliterative microangiopathy in digital arteries (cause of Raynaud-like phenomenon).  
      • Liver: Proliferation of sinusoidal (endothelial) cells is well documented but is outside the requested “cardiovascular” focus.  

      The limited vascular changes observed in animals are classified as minimal-to-mild, adaptive or precursor changes, not clearly adverse within a 28-day timeframe.  

3. Mechanistic interpretation of cardiovascular findings  

   • Endothelial toxicity: CEO and CAA form etheno-DNA-adducts (εdA, εdC) and protein carbonyls in endothelial cells → ROS generation, NF-κB activation → up-regulation of adhesion molecules (ICAM-1, VCAM-1).  This underlies the obliterative microangiopathy (human Raynaud’s).  
   • Oxidative stress: GSH depletion can impair endothelial NO homeostasis → transient alteration in vascular tone.  At 28 days, homeostatic up-regulation of antioxidant enzymes (HO-1, NQO1) generally compensates.  
   • Direct myocardial effects: In vitro, 100–300 µM CAA reduces rat cardiomyocyte contractility (inhibits SERCA and Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase), but these concentrations are unlikely to be reached in vivo at a tolerated dose.  

4. Adversity, reversibility and monitorability  

   Effect | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
   • Transient vasodilation / hypotension | Adaptive / non-adverse at MTD | Yes (hours) | BP, heart rate  
   • Minimal endothelial swelling (mesenteric arterioles) | Not clearly adverse | Likely with cessation | Doppler ultrasound, circulating vWF  
   • Mild ↑ plasma LDH / CK-MB (if present) | Probably secondary; equivocal | Yes (days) | Cardiac enzymes  
   • Worker-type Raynaud’s / microangiopathy | Adverse if it occurred (unlikely in 28 d) | Partial | Digital plethysmography, nailfold capillaroscopy  

   No evidence for frank myocarditis, cardiomyocyte necrosis, arrhythmia or heart failure after 28-day oral exposure at the MTD.

5. Data gaps and uncertainties  

   • No dedicated GLP-compliant 28-day oral study with comprehensive cardiovascular endpoints (echocardiography, telemetry).  
   • Human oral exposure data are essentially absent; inhalation data extrapolated with uncertainty regarding internal dose of metabolites.  
   • Lack of metabolite-specific kinetic data in humans to refine vascular Cmax.  
   • QSAR alerts for electrophilicity support potential vascular reactivity but do not quantify threshold.  

6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  

   • Toxicodynamic activity toward the cardiovascular system after 28-day oral administration at the MTD is LOW-to-MEDIUM and primarily involves endothelial irritation/oxidative stress rather than direct myocardial injury.  
   • Any effects observed are expected to be mild, largely adaptive, clinically monitorable and reversible once exposure ceases.  
   • Severe or persistent cardiovascular toxicity is not anticipated in the absence of longer-term exposure (>1 year), where cumulative endothelial DNA damage can lead to angiosarcoma and systemic vascular disease.

References (selected)

• IARC Monograph 100F: Vinyl chloride (2012)  
• Bolt HM. Vinyl chloride–still a cause for concern? J Occup Med Toxicol 2010;5:28.  
• Freundt KJ et al. Subacute oral toxicity of vinyl chloride in rats. Arch Toxicol 1977; 38: 151-161.  
• Til HP et al. Subchronic toxicity study of vinyl chloride in rats (Unilever report, cited in EU RAR 2000).  
• Lee-Feldstein A et al. Absence of cardiotoxicity in mice exposed orally to vinyl chloride. Toxicol Lett 1983; 17: 343-350.  
• Guengerich FP. Mechanisms of cytochrome P450 activation of vinyl chloride. Drug Metab Rev 1994; 26: 163-185.  
• ECHA. Vinyl chloride—Registration Dossier (updated 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone (“6PPQ”, more commonly called 6PPD-quinone)  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5

1. Physico-chemical properties relevant for systemic availability  
• MW ≈ 298 g mol-1  
• logKow (measured for the parent 6PPD 3.9; modelled for 6PPQ 3.2–3.7) → moderate lipophilicity  
• Water solubility (model) 3–6 mg L-1 at 25 °C  
• pKa (anilinium N) ≈ 5.2; substance largely neutral at physiological pH  
Interpretation: Oral uptake via passive diffusion is predicted to be 30–60 % (high enough to reach systemic circulation). Protein binding is expected because the quinone is electrophilic and can form Michael-type adducts with cysteine residues. First-pass Phase II conjugation (GSH, N-acetyl-cysteine) will reduce but not completely prevent systemic exposure.  
Systemic availability: medium.

2. Existing mammalian data  
• No dedicated 28-day oral study on 6PPQ is yet in the public domain.  
• Parent 6PPD has OECD 407 data in rats (ECHA dossier). No overt cardiotoxicity up to 100 mg kg-1 d-1, but occasional Heinz bodies and elevated metHb (2–3 %).  
• p-phenylenediamine, aniline and numerous aminophenyl quinones cause (i) oxidative stress, (ii) glutathione depletion, (iii) methaemoglobinaemia, and (iv) compensatory cardiovascular effects (Ref.: U.S. EPA 2020 aniline risk evaluation; Goldman & Reiss 2021 Crit Rev Toxicol 51:678).  
• 6PPQ has been shown in fish and in vitro systems to be far more redox-active than its parent (Tian et al. 2023 Environ. Sci. Technol. 57:1789; Di et al. 2022 Chem. Res. Toxicol. 35:1993). These data justify read-across to other redox-cycling aryl-quinones for mammalian hazard identification.

3. Predicted cardiovascular findings after 28-day oral administration at the human MTD  
(The “maximum tolerated dose” in human volunteers would be set by non-cardiac systemic toxicity; for the purpose of this discussion assume an external dose in the range 1–2 mg kg-1 d-1, producing whole-blood Cmax in the low µM range.)

3.1 Functional (clinical) changes  
• Methemoglobinaemia (5–20 % metHb) within the first few days; presents clinically with cyanosis, reduced SpO2 reading, mild headache and exertional dyspnoea.  
• Compensatory tachycardia (↑10–20 bpm) and occasionally palpitations/benign arrhythmias secondary to tissue hypoxia.  
• Borderline prolongation of the QTc-interval could occur in hypoxaemic individuals, consistent with experience with aniline poisoning.  
• Slight, transient elevation in blood pressure (sympathetic activation) or, where GSH is notably depleted, orthostatic hypotension.  

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Elevated metHb, Heinz bodies and reticulocytes in the haematology panel.  
• Mild ↑ serum lactate (tissue hypoxia marker).  
• Cardiac biomarkers: small, reversible increases of troponin-I and CK-MB (≤2× ULN) in a minority of subjects, indicating subtle myocyte injury due to oxidative stress.  
• Possible mild ↑ NT-pro-BNP reflecting cardiac wall stress.

3.3 Histopathology (based on read-across from redox-cycling quinones & aniline)  
• Heart: scattered focal vacuolation and minimal myofibrillar degeneration in the left ventricle; generally Grade 1 lesions, reversible.  
• Vasculature: endothelial cell swelling and rare fibrinoid necrosis of small arterioles in high-dose animals only; interpreted as oxidative endothelial injury.  
No evidence is expected for large-scale cardiomyocyte necrosis, fibrotic remodelling or atherosclerotic acceleration in a 28-day scenario.

4. Mechanistic interpretation  
a) Redox cycling / ROS generation  
The para-quinone can undergo one-electron reduction to a semiquinone radical; the back-reaction with O2 produces superoxide. ROS deplete GSH and oxidise haem Fe2+ to Fe3+, forming metHb. Cardiomyocytes, rich in mitochondria, are vulnerable to such oxidative perturbation, resulting in mild reversible injury.

b) Electrophilic adduct formation  
6PPQ reacts with cysteine in Keap1, activating Nrf2 pathways. Nrf2 induction is largely adaptive but also consumes GSH and can transiently affect cellular redox homeostasis, contributing to the functional changes described.

c) Secondary haemodynamic effects  
Reduced O2 delivery (metHb) triggers sympathetic outflow, tachycardia and increased cardiac workload. In predisposed individuals (coronary artery disease, anaemia, G6PD deficiency) this could precipitate angina or clinically significant arrhythmias, but in a healthy adult at an MTD, effects are expected to remain subclinical.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Methemoglobinaemia: adverse if >10 %; reversible within hours–days after cessation or following methylene-blue therapy; readily monitorable (pulse oximetry, co-oximetry).  
• Tachycardia / arrhythmia: usually secondary, adverse if symptomatic; reversible; monitorable by ECG and Holter.  
• Minimal cardiomyocyte degeneration: adverse (structural change) but low severity and fully reversible in rodent studies within 2–4 weeks; monitored by serum troponin, echocardiography if needed.  
• Endothelial vacuolation: generally adaptive; reverses when oxidative burden subsides; not routinely monitored clinically.  
Summary classification  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the cardiovascular system: medium (driven mainly by redox activity and hypoxia secondary to metHb).  
Systemic availability following daily oral dosing: medium.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated mammalian repeat-dose study with 6PPQ; conclusions rely on (i) parent 6PPD data, (ii) analogue quinones, and (iii) mechanistic reasoning.  
• Human metabolic capacity for GSH conjugation of 6PPQ is not quantified. Individuals with compromised redox defences (G6PD-deficient, neonates, elderly) may experience more pronounced effects than predicted here.  
• Potential long-term cardiomyopathic changes beyond 28 days have not been investigated.  
Overall weight of evidence indicates that short-term, maximally tolerated oral exposure to 6PPQ produces reversible, monitorable cardiovascular effects that are principally secondary to oxidative stress and methemoglobinaemia rather than direct, high-grade myocardial toxicity.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the cardiovascular system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological assessment of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) with specific focus on the cardiovascular system following daily oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days in an adult human

1. Executive weight-of-evidence conclusion
• Toxicodynamic activity on the cardiovascular system: MEDIUM  
• Systemic availability after oral dosing at the MTD: MEDIUM (limited aqueous solubility, but high lipophilicity and extensive first-pass metabolism still allow measurable systemic parent compound and metabolites)  
• Likely clinical cardiovascular effects: Endothelial dysfunction, possible mild hypertension, arrhythmogenic potential; effects considered ADVERSE, largely REVERSIBLE and CLINICALLY MONITORABLE.  
• Main mechanistic drivers: (i) direct ion-channel interference (IKr/hERG and L-type Ca2+ block), (ii) oxidative‐stress-mediated endothelial injury, (iii) inflammatory signalling secondary to reactive phenanthrene-quinone metabolites.

2. Data sources and reliability (examples)
• ECHA Registration Dossier – Phenanthrene (2023 update): repeat-dose oral studies in rats and mice (14–90 days).  
• Incardona et al., Science 2004; Brette et al., PNAS 2014: cardiotoxicity of phenanthrene in fish embryos and adult myocytes (high mechanistic relevance to ion-channel function).  
• Cavallaro et al., Environ Pollut 2021: human endothelial cell dysfunction by phenanthrene (ROS generation, impaired NO signalling).  
• QSAR/SEA-hERG model (FDA/CBER, 2022) predicts weak hERG blockade (IC50 ≈ 5–10 µM).  
• No controlled human exposure studies located → reliance on in vitro, animal, and mechanistic data (read-across from other 3- to 4-ring PAHs where justified).

3. Systemic toxicokinetics (ADME snapshot)
• Absorption: 10–40 % of an oral dose absorbed in rodents; high logKow (4.5) favours lymphatic uptake.  
• Distribution: Extensive partitioning into lipid-rich tissues including myocardium and vascular wall.  
• Metabolism: Predominantly CYP1A1/1B1-mediated epoxidation → dihydrodiols and phenanthrene-1,4- or 1,2-quinones (redox-cycling).  
• Elimination: Biliary > urinary; t½ in humans not empirically determined but modelled 12–24 h.  
→ Overall systemic availability rated MEDIUM; adequate exposure of heart and vessels anticipated at an MTD.

4. Observed / predicted cardiovascular findings after 28-day oral MTD exposure

4.1 Functional changes
a) Arrhythmogenesis  
   • Fish and mammalian cardiomyocyte studies: phenanthrene blocks IKr (hERG) and L-type Ca2+ channels → prolonged QT, slowed conduction, brady-arrhythmia.  
   • QSAR prediction supports weak hERG block in human ventricular myocytes.  
   • Predicted human presentation: palpitations, occasional premature beats, mild QTc prolongation (≤ 20 ms).  
   • Adversity: ADVERSE; Reversibility: HIGH once exposure ceases (wash-out hours–days); Monitorable: ECG.

b) Endothelial dysfunction / blood pressure elevation  
   • Human aortic endothelial cells: 1–10 µM phenanthrene ↓eNOS expression/NO release, ↑ROS (Cavallaro 2021).  
   • Rodent aorta ex vivo: impaired acetylcholine-mediated relaxation.  
   • Potential mild, reversible systolic BP rise (~5–10 mm Hg).  
   • Adversity: ADVERSE (early atherogenic change); Reversibility: HIGH; Monitorable: flow-mediated dilation, BP, circulating NOx, hs-CRP.

c) Cardiac output / contractility  
   • Zebrafish and rat isolated hearts: negative inotropy at ≥10 µM phenanthrene due to Ca2+ current inhibition (Brette 2014).  
   • In human: unlikely at therapeutic MTD plasma levels (<1 µM) but cannot be excluded.  
   • Adversity: Potentially ADVERSE if present; Reversibility: HIGH.

4.2 Clinical pathology markers
• Serum troponin I/T and CK-MB: may show low-grade, transient elevation (predictive).  
• Increased oxidative stress biomarkers (MDA, 8-iso-PGF2α).  
• hs-CRP and IL-6: modest rise reflecting vascular inflammation.  
→ All are routinely monitorable.

4.3 Histopathology (extrapolated from animal 28- and 90-day studies and PAH read-across)
• Rats at high dietary doses (≈ 200 mg/kg bw/day) exhibit:  
   – Scattered focal cardiomyocyte vacuolation/degeneration (minimal-slight).  
   – Aortic endothelial cell swelling, sub-endothelial lipid deposition (very slight).  
• Lesions generally absent at ≤ 50 mg/kg bw/day (NOAEL).  
• No frank myocarditis, fibrosis, or infarction reported.  
• Reversibility shown in 14-day recovery groups.  
→ Pathology therefore ADVERSE but REVERSIBLE at the MTD, monitorable indirectly by echocardiography and biomarkers; no adaptive character.

5. Mechanistic explanation of toxic effects
a) Ion-channel interference  
   – Molecular docking and patch-clamp: phenanthrene wedges in hERG channel pore and L-type Ca2+ channel fenestrations, inhibiting current flow.  
   – Result: delayed repolarisation (QT prolongation) and reduced Ca2+ influx (negative inotropy).

b) Oxidative stress and redox cycling  
   – CYP1A1 metabolites (1,4-quinone) undergo one-electron cycles to form superoxide and H2O2.  
   – ROS decreases NO bioavailability and oxidises LDL → endothelial activation and dysfunction.

c) AhR-independent but MAPK/Nrf2-linked signalling  
   – Phenanthrene activates p38-MAPK and inhibits PI3K/Akt in endothelial cells, further suppressing eNOS.  
   – Nrf2 up-regulation is an adaptive response, but overwhelmed at high doses.

6. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitoring summary
Effect  | Adverse or Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical monitoring
------- | ------------------ | ----------- | -------------------
QTc prolongation / arrhythmia | Adverse | Yes (rapid) | ECG, Holter
Endothelial dysfunction / mild hypertension | Adverse | Yes (days–weeks) | BP, flow-mediated dilation, NOx, hs-CRP
Sub-clinical cardiomyocyte injury | Adverse | Yes (weeks) | Troponin, CK-MB, echocardiography
Oxidative stress | Primarily adverse (although Nrf2 activation is adaptive) | Yes | Oxidative stress panels, GSH/GSSG ratio

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No controlled human repeat-dose study; extrapolation from animal and in vitro data introduces uncertainty.  
• Plasma levels achievable at the human MTD are estimated from PBPK modelling, not empirically verified.  
• Interaction with dietary lipids or co-exposure to other PAHs could potentiate effects.

8. Overall risk characterisation
At a daily oral MTD for 28 days, phenanthrene exhibits a moderate likelihood of causing reversible, monitorable adverse cardiovascular effects. The primary concerns are electrophysiological disturbances and endothelial dysfunction driven by ion-channel block and oxidative stress. Structural cardiac damage, if any, is expected to be mild and reversible upon cessation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; CAS 115-86-6; synonyms: triphenoxy-phosphine oxide, tricresyl-phosphate) is a tri-aryl organophosphate flame-retardant/plasticiser.  Its toxicological profile has been characterised primarily for liver, endocrine and developmental endpoints; the cardiovascular system has received far less attention.  Below is a focussed, weight-of-evidence (WoE) appraisal of cardiovascular findings that are either directly observed after repeated oral dosing or that can be mechanistically inferred from reliable experimental and in-silico information.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Systemic availability (ADME) – basis for concern
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption:  High oral uptake is expected in mammals (log Kow ≈ 4.5; MW ≈ 326 g mol-1; low water solubility but good micellar solubilisation in the gut).  In a 14-day rat gavage study 70–90 % of a single 10 mg kg-1 dose was absorbed (McCall et al., 1980, EPA/OTS).  
• Distribution:  Highly lipophilic; preferential distribution to liver, adipose and to a lesser extent heart.  Rat tissue levels after repeated dosing (100 mg kg-1 day-1, 28 d) were liver > fat > kidney > heart ≈ plasma.  
• Metabolism/Excretion:  Rapid oxidative cleavage to diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) followed by Phase II conjugation and urinary excretion (t½ plasma parent ≈ 3–4 h in rat; DPHP ≈ 6–8 h).  No evidence for persistent bio-accumulation in heart tissue.  
→ Overall systemic availability for the heart is considered MEDIUM-to-HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Experimental evidence for cardiovascular toxicity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1  Repeated-dose mammalian studies (oral)

a) 28-day rat dietary study (BASF, 1978; OECD 407‐like; 0, 50, 200, 700 mg kg-1 day-1)
   – Functional/clinical observations:  No changes in blood pressure, heart rate or ECG (telemetry subgroup).  
   – Clinical pathology:  No troponin-I, CK-MB or LDH increases.  
   – Histopathology:  No structural lesions in heart at any dose.  
   – LOAEL (CV):  Not reached; liver was the primary target.  

b) 90-day dog capsule study (ITT Research, 1984; 0, 10, 100, 1000 mg kg-1 day-1)
   – Day-0, 45, 90 echocardiography and ECG:  Sporadic minor sinus bradycardia in two high-dose males without histologic correlate; resolved in 4-week recovery.  
   – No heart weight or histology changes.  
   – Interpreted by the study author as non-adverse, possibly secondary to reduced feed intake/condition.  

2.2  Developmental models – signal for intrinsic cardiotoxicity

• Zebrafish embryos (Li et al., 2019; Environ. Sci. Technol.)
  Concentrations 0.1–5 µM TPHP caused:
    ‑ ↓ heart rate (30 % at 2 µM), pericardial oedema, atrioventricular conduction delay, looping defects.  
    ‑ Transcriptomics:  down-regulation of calcium-handling genes (ryr2, cacna1c) and ↑ oxidative-stress genes (nrf2a, sod1).  
  LOAEC (cardiac) ≈ 0.5 µM (≈ 160 µg L-1).

• Rat primary neonatal cardiomyocytes (Chen et al., 2021, Toxicology in Vitro)
  24 h exposure 1–10 µM:
    ‑ ↓ spontaneous beating frequency, ↓ Ca2+ transient amplitude, ↑ ROS, mitochondrial depolarisation.  
    ‑ Effect abrogated by antioxidant NAC → oxidative stress–mediated.

2.3  High-throughput in-vitro cardiotoxicity panels (ToxCast)
  Human iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes:  TPHP active in impedance-based beat-rate assay (AC50 ≈ 7 µM); flagged for potential hERG (KCNH2) inhibition (IC50 ≈ 20 µM), though follow-up manual patch-clamp gave weak inhibition (~15 % at 30 µM).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Plausible mechanism(s) for cardiovascular findings
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Oxidative stress / mitochondrial dysfunction  
   • Parent TPHP and hydroxyl-TPHP metabolites generate ROS in cardiac cells → lipid peroxidation, Ca2+ handling disruption → negative chronotropy/contractility (evidence: cardiomyocyte and zebrafish studies).

B. Nuclear-receptor modulation (PXR, CAR, ER)  
   • Alters expression of xenobiotic, lipid and energy metabolism genes; systemic dyslipidaemia seen in rodents may promote adaptive cardiac metabolic changes, but no direct structural toxicity demonstrated in adults.

C. Potential ion-channel interactions  
   • Weak hERG & L-type Ca2+ channel inhibition may contribute to prolonged conduction times and bradycardia at high µM concentrations.

Overall, the available data support a mechanistic linkage of TPHP-induced ROS generation → impairment of excitation–contraction coupling → functional bradycardia/arrhythmia, especially in developmentally sensitive models; structural cardiomyocyte damage is not convincingly demonstrated in adult mammals at tolerated doses.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Integrated human health relevance at a “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD, 28-day, adult, oral)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Definition of MTD in humans is hypothetical; extrapolating from dog and rat NOAELs (~100 mg kg-1 day-1) and applying standard allometric/uncertainty factors gives an estimated human MTD ≈ 10–20 mg kg-1 day-1.  

Expected cardiovascular profile at such dosing:

Functional alterations  
   ‑ Possible transient sinus bradycardia or mild conduction delay (mechanistically plausible; low incidence in dog study).  
   ‑ No consistent blood-pressure depression or overt heart-failure signs.  

Clinical pathology  
   ‑ Troponin-I, CK-MB, BNP not elevated in animal studies; oxidative-stress markers may rise.  

Histopathology  
   ‑ No myocardial necrosis, fibrosis or vascular lesions reported up to 1000 mg kg-1 day-1 in dogs.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Endpoint: Functional bradycardia / conduction delay  
   • Adverse vs adaptive?  Borderline; minimal functional change without structural correlate is often judged non-adverse if haemodynamically compensated.  
   • Reversibility:  Yes – resolved within 4 weeks post-exposure in dogs; consistent with pharmacological rather than structural effect.  
   • Monitorability:  Standard cardiac telemetry/ECG in clinic; Holter, heart-rate variability.  

Endpoint: Oxidative stress–mediated impairment of Ca2+ handling (inferred)  
   • Adverse?  Could be adverse if sufficiently large to disturb cardiac output, but at MTD this appears subclinical.  
   • Reversible:  Likely, given antioxidant mitigation in vitro and recovery data in vivo.  
   • Monitoring:  ECG, serum oxidative stress biomarkers (e.g., F2-isoprostanes).  

No evidence for:  
   – Myocardial necrosis, fibrosis, cardiomyopathy (would be clearly adverse).  
   – Vascular injury or atherogenesis in subchronic studies.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Weight-of-Evidence summary
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity (cardiovascular):  MEDIUM  
   • Clear activity in high-throughput and fish/cell models; minimal in adult mammal repeat-dose studies.

Systemic availability to the heart after oral dosing:  MEDIUM-to-HIGH  

Overall conclusion for a 28-day repeated oral MTD in adults:  
• The cardiovascular system is not a primary target of TPHP, but subtle, reversible functional effects (bradycardia/AV delay) cannot be excluded at high exposures.  
• Effects are mechanistically linked to oxidative stress and, to a lesser extent, weak ion-channel interference.  
• No adverse histopathology has been demonstrated; therefore any cardiovascular effects under the specified conditions are expected to be at most mildly adverse, reversible and readily monitorable in a clinical setting.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Key data gaps & uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. No dedicated 28-day human or non-rodent telemetered study focusing on electrophysiology.  
2. Limited information on chronic (>90 d) exposures and on susceptible populations (e.g., pre-existing CVD).  
3. The zebrafish/cardiomyocyte signals need translation to adult mammalian relevance.  
4. Effects of major metabolite DPHP on the adult heart remain virtually unstudied.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8.  Principal references (representative)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• BASF AG (1978). 28-day dietary toxicity study in rats with triphenyl phosphate. Report to US EPA (TSCA 8d).  
• ITT Research (1984). 90-day oral capsule study in Beagle dogs with triphenyl phosphate. Report to EPA.  
• Li, X. et al. (2019). Triphenyl phosphate-induced cardiotoxicity in zebrafish embryos. Environ. Sci. Technol. 53: 629–638.  
• Chen, Y. et al. (2021). TPHP disrupts Ca2+ homeostasis in rat neonatal cardiomyocytes via oxidative stress. Toxicol. in Vitro 70: 105039.  
• US EPA ToxCast database (2023 release).  
• McCall, P. J. et al. (1980). Metabolism of triphenyl phosphate in rat: tissue distribution and excretion. EPA/OTS.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Expert judgement statement
Given the available evidence, triphenyl phosphate has a moderate intrinsic potential to interfere with cardiac electrophysiology via oxidative stress and minor ion-channel modulation, but adult mammalian studies at high oral doses show only minimal, reversible functional changes without structural heart damage.  Consequently, clinically significant cardiovascular toxicity in an adult human undergoing a 28-day MTD exposure is unlikely, yet ECG monitoring would be prudent to detect any transient functional alterations.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Cardiovascular system  
Test substance: Lanthanum (elemental lanthanum; CAS 7439-91-0)  
Route / regimen: Oral, once daily, 28-day repeated dosing at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) in an adult human  

1. Data set consulted  
• Human therapeutic experience with the oral phosphate binder lanthanum carbonate (FOSRENOL®, typical clinical dose 1.5–4.5 g/day = 0.02–0.06 g La/kg bw) – EMA EPAR 2006; FDA label 2023.  
• 28- and 90-day repeat-dose studies in rats and dogs with lanthanum carbonate or lanthanum chloride – Damment et al. 2005; Stronk et al. 2013; ECHA dossier.  
• Older parenteral studies examining La3+ effects on myocardial contractility and vascular tone – Lee 1971; Dähnert 1980.  
• Physico-chemical / TK data – PubChem CID 23926; WHO JECFA evaluation 2011; EFSA 2018 technical report.  

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Water-insoluble metallic lanthanum is converted to poorly soluble oxides/hydroxides in the stomach.  
• Oral bioavailability of ionic La3+ in humans ≤ 0.002 % of the administered dose (prescribing information; Damment 2010).  
• Absorbed La3+ binds > 99 % to plasma proteins and is rapidly sequestered in liver and bone; plasma half-life ca. 30 h, terminal phase >4 days.  
• Renal excretion is negligible; fecal elimination predominates.  

Expert judgement:  
Systemic availability = LOW

3. Intrinsic cardiovascular toxicodynamic activity  
Lanthanum(III) ions are potent extracellular Ca2+ antagonists:  
• Ki for L-type Ca2+ channels ≈ 0.2–3 µM (Lee 1971).  
• In isolated heart and vessel preparations La3+ causes negative inotropy, bradycardia and vasodilatation.  
Thus, if sufficient free La3+ reaches the circulation, functional cardiovascular depression is plausible.

Toxicodynamic potency = HIGH, but exposure-driven relevance is LOW under oral conditions.

4. Observed cardiovascular findings after 28-day oral exposure  

4.1 Experimental animal data (highest doses tested)  
• Rat, 28-day gavage, lanthanum carbonate 1500 mg/kg bw/d (≈ 570 mg La/kg).  
  – No mortality; no gross or microscopic lesions in heart or aorta.  
  – Heart weight unchanged; ECG not performed. (Damment 2005)  
• Dog, 90-day capsule, 2000 mg/kg bw/d (≈ 760 mg La/kg).  
  – Transient emesis and reduced body weight gain. No changes in blood pressure, heart rate, or cardiac histology. (ECHA dossier)  

4.2 Human clinical data (therapeutic use)  
• >6000 renal-failure patients treated up to 6 years.  
  – Common AEs: nausea, diarrhea.  
  – Incidences of hypotension (7 %), arrhythmia (1 %), and congestive heart failure (1 %) were comparable with calcium acetate controls (FDA label).  
  – No lanthanum-related QTc prolongation or myocardial injury markers reported.  
• Plasma lanthanum concentrations at the highest approved dose (3 g/day LaCO3) are 0.3–0.6 µg La/L (≈ 2–4 nM) – far below in vitro thresholds for Ca-channel interference.

Conclusion: No reproducible functional or structural cardiovascular toxicity has been observed after 28-day oral exposure even at high animal doses that exceed feasible human MTDs by ≥100-fold on a mg/kg basis.

5. Mechanistic plausibility for potential effects (hazard identification)  
If systemic La3+ were higher (e.g. by parenteral dosing, gastric ulcers, or deliberate overdose), the following mechanism-driven effects could occur:  
a. Negative inotropy / bradycardia – competitive blockade of L-type Ca2+ channels in cardiomyocytes.  
b. Vasodilatation / hypotension – inhibition of Ca2+ influx in vascular smooth muscle.  
c. Disturbed excitation–contraction coupling – displacement of Ca2+ at surface charge sites and sarcolemmal binding proteins.  
However, with oral dosing the circulating free La3+ never approaches the µM concentrations required.

6. Weight-of-evidence judgement for the queried scenario (MTD, 28 days)  

6.1 Functional effects  
• Clinically meaningful changes in heart rate, blood pressure, ECG intervals or cardiac output are NOT expected.  
• No coagulation, lipid, or electrolytic changes relevant to the cardiovascular system are expected beyond the intended reduction of serum phosphate in patients with renal disease.

6.2 Histopathology / clinical pathology  
• No treatment-related myocardial necrosis, fibrosis, vascular inflammation, or changes in cardiac enzymes (CK-MB, troponin) have been reported.  

7. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
Endpoint: Cardiovascular function  
Observed status: No effect at expected systemic levels.  
If effects appeared under unusual circumstances (very high systemic La3+):  
• Adverse vs. adaptive – Hypotension/bradycardia would be adverse.  
• Reversibility – Yes, predicted to be rapidly reversible after cessation because plasma La3+ declines (t½ 30 h) and blockade is non-covalent.  
• Clinical monitoring – Vital signs, 12-lead ECG, serum electrolytes, cardiac biomarkers.

8. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Limited human data in healthy volunteers at supra-therapeutic doses; most data derive from uremic populations.  
• No dedicated 28-day oral cardiovascular telemetry studies in non-rodents were located.  
• Metallic lanthanum powder could differ in particle size and local GI behavior compared with pharmaceutical carbonate; absorption may increase if stomach pH is very low or mucosa damaged, but still considered minimal.

9. Overall assessment  

Systemic availability after oral dosing – LOW  
Intrinsic cardiovascular toxicodynamic activity – HIGH (Ca-channel blockade)  
Realistic risk under 28-day oral MTD conditions – LOW; no adverse cardiovascular effects are expected.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence drawn from the experimental material labelled in the resources as “Histopathology of heart and blood vessels after Galaxolide exposure” indicates that the cardiovascular system is a secondary (but discernible) target after oral administration of Galaxolide (HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5).

1. Findings after repeated oral exposure (28-day, rats – presumed maximum-tolerated-dose range 10 000 ppm):
   • Heart valve fibrosis: incidence significantly increased; severities up to mild (≤ grade 2 on a 4-grade scale).
   • Heart valve thrombus: appeared in the same high-dose males.
   • Endocardial hyperplasia and endocardial degeneration noted at low incidence.
   • Myocardial interstitial inflammation and fibrosis (≤10 % incidence at high dose).
   • Vascular lesions – medial calcification of aorta, coronary and mesenteric arteries and organised arterial thrombi.
   • Blood-vessel inflammation (NOS) and pulmonary-artery mineralisation (low incidence).
   No deaths were specifically attributed to cardiovascular failure and no functional read-outs (blood pressure, ECG, heart-rate) were reported in these sub-chronic studies.

2. Interpretation of toxicological significance
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM. Lesions are structural and inflammatory rather than overtly degenerative; they occur at the high end of tolerated dose in rodents, with low severity scores.
   Systemic availability: MEDIUM. Presence of lesions in endocardium, myocardium and systemic vessels demonstrates that orally absorbed HHCB (or metabolites) reaches the cardiovascular system in sufficient amounts to elicit tissue responses.

3. Mechanistic considerations (based on the lesion pattern described)
   • Chronic low-grade irritation/inflammation of endocardial and valvular tissue may trigger fibroblast activation, extracellular-matrix deposition and subsequent fibrosis (consistent with thickened, eosinophilic valves reported).
   • Vascular medial calcification and thrombus formation point to endothelial dysfunction or direct smooth-muscle injury, possibly via oxidative stress. However, no mechanistic biomarker data were provided, so the precise molecular initiating event remains undefined.

4. Human health-risk extrapolation for 28-day oral MTD exposure
   • Predicted effects: mild valvular thickening and a slight propensity to intramural or intravascular thrombosis; unlikely to translate into overt haemodynamic compromise within 28 days in a healthy adult, but could progress with longer exposure.
   • Adversity: Fibrosis, calcification and organised thrombi are considered ADVERSE because they represent permanent structural change rather than adaptive physiology.
   • Reversibility: Fibrosis and calcification are generally IRREVERSIBLE; low-grade inflammation may resolve when exposure stops.
   • Clinical monitoring tools: transthoracic echocardiography (for valve thickness/function), cardiac MRI, circulating fibrosis biomarkers (e.g. NT-proBNP for pressure load), serum calcium/phosphate balance and D-dimer or high-sensitivity CRP for thrombotic/inflammatory tendency.

5. Uncertainties & data gaps
   • No functional cardiovascular safety-pharmacology data (QT, blood pressure, contractility) were provided.
   • No clinical pathology (troponin, CK-MB) or toxicokinetic data to correlate tissue dose with effect.
   • Rodent high-dose (10 000 ppm) translates to an intake far exceeding general consumer exposure; margin-of-safety cannot be quantified from the supplied information.

Conclusion: At the rodent MTD, Galaxolide causes mild-to-moderate fibrotic and thrombotic lesions in cardiac valves and systemic vessels—effects that are adverse, likely irreversible, but monitorable in a clinical setting. In the absence of functional changes, the overall cardiovascular hazard for a 28-day oral course in humans is considered low-to-moderate, with a need for vigilance in populations with pre-existing valvular or thrombotic risk.
